speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four, Dorrit St. John.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I arrived in California. And I knew that my aunt was here. There was a cot in the living room that I can open up. And I knew I could put my body somewhere with my child. Everything that I had gathered over the 11 years, I left to my ex-husband. He had no idea I left. He didn't know. I left a coffee pot on the stove. So, he couldn't-- he had no idea that I was going to be gone anywhere. But my life with him became so unbearable that, what we have now, the abused woman syndrome, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went through that, with the only exception he never laid a hand on me physically. But the verbal and the mental abuse was worse than I could ever explain. And this is why I said, there's got to be another life. And I-- my parents finally conceded to the fact. 'Go, my child. Yes, there will be another life for you.' Because you know, in 1959, a divorced woman was a tainted woman, completely tainted. That meant [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you couldn't get insurance. You couldn't get your own credit. There was nothing you could do. Everything had to be on the husband's name. And what I did was, when I came here, I immediately looked for a job, the second day around, as an operator on a sewing machine, sewing draperies. And I figured as long as I can feed myself and my son, I won't worry about anything. And I also had to apply-- I applied for divorce, which you couldn't get. You had to have residency in California for one year. So I filed for a separate maintenance and a restraining order. Because, I was, by that time, it had reached such proportions that I was fearful of my life. And I went to work in a drapery shop. And, lo and behold, there was this crazy guy. He was one of the partners in that shop that used to jump in and jump out. And he was-- he was the outside installer of the draperies. And I was one of the girls that was stitching the drapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to somebody, who is this crazy guy? Oh, he says, that's Jerry St. John. He's the partner of the guy that-- they run this little shop together, you know, And I said, OK. So finally, about after the third or fourth day there, he came over to me and said, what's your name? And I said, it's Dorrit. He says, I can't remember that, I'll just call you Duchess. And that was the end of it. So I said, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: To make a long story short, we started to keep company-- very distant, but company. Very proper and all that. Because, at that time, you didn't do any [? kinkala-diminkalas ?]. You don't do those things. We kept company. And after about six or seven months, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jerry said to me he would like me to consider to get married. I said, absolutely not. I don't want to get married. I am not even-- I don't want to think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, after a year, I could file for divorce. And I filed for divorce a year later. In the meantime, my ex-husband found me. And he thought he-- I was a missing person, but somehow he found out where I was. And I, I shared a house with another young divorced woman who had a child, so we shared the expenses.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you can always find a way how to live, as long as, you know, you make a buck and you can live. And we shared the house together. And he came there at night, and he practically busted down the whole house. I had to call the police, because I had the restraining order. So I was able to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] make him go away. And my son, it was very, very difficult for him to understand this whole thing. Very difficult experience. I tried the best I knew how to work, to take care of him. But it was-- I wasn't a housewife with the apron on that could just stay home and take care of a child. So, there are, you know, you can't put guilts on yourself. You had to survive. I had to feed my child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I always felt guilty I wasn't doing enough. How can I-- you know, what can I do, to make sure that the child is also happy? Very difficult. Bring us up to date to the present. You know--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Make it-- and then we do the fifth [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we finally did get married--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Three years later. And we went into business together. And Jerry, my husband, had two-- two daughters from a previous marriage. And, of course, here I was, raising three kids, having difficulty raising one. But I was young, stupid, and very much in love. And I did it. And I started to induce a lot of Jewishness into the girls, who didn't have that before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your second husband was Christian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But he was-- he had no religion. He was totally without religion. He wanted to turn to Judaism if this is what I wanted. And I said, I'll take you the way you are, whether you are Jewish or not. And he can talk Jewish better than I can. And so, religion never, ever played a role. But he did know my Jewish heritage and how strong I believed in that. And I induced that into the children, the high moral standards. I imposed very high moral standards. And-- and my daughter, my oldest daughter, which is my-- my step-daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Step-daughter, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Became-- she went through the bat mitzvah, and she became a Jew. And she married a Jew. And, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: She converted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her-- of course, her daughter is Jewish. And she's a better Jew than I will ever be. And the other daughter also married a Jewish man, and they have a child. And my son married a young lady who also turned to Judaism. And we have five granddaughters that my son gave us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does your son do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is in a similar business that I used to have. He is a purchasing manager for a company. And, as I went on, I finally went into a business about 20 years ago that was buying-- a buying company. And I was buying interior furnishings for hotels. I had a purchasing company. And we had offices. I had partners and a very large design company and interior designers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you're still working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I quit a year ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aha.

speaker: interviewee
text: I quit. I have become a lady of leisure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, finally.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, in the 20 years-- finally-- I had tremendous success in our business. And I opened up offices in London, in Hong Kong, in Atlanta. And so I was always on the go. I was traveling year-round in the business. And business grew, became very successful. And I'm happy to say that I received many awards within our industry, which I am absolutely [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] beyond expression what it meant to me. I was named woman of the year in our industry. I organized an organization for women in 1984, which is called Network of Executive Women in Hospitality. I was the founder of that. We now have 1,400 members in, in 10 different states in the United States, 10 chapters. And it's-- I feel tremendous satisfaction of accomplishment. And I got the Platinum Circle Award, which is given in our industry for outstanding people. And basically, I'm a very, very happy and fully fulfilled person in my life. I have the best husband in the world. We have a wonderful life together. The children are great. The grandchildren are great. And I just told them, don't come to grandma babysitting! She is not a babysitter!

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. OK. Grandpapa Lissianski, my mother's father, and her youngest brother, Sasha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Vienna, in 1910. Mama and Papa, 1918, the-- the year they got married in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1924, the year I was born. I was three months old. And next to me is my sister Liesl, who was about a year and a half at that time. My mother, my sister, and myself, in 1927, in Vienna, as we were growing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1927. [LAUGHTER] Mama and Papa in their business, my father being an importer, in 1952 in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: I start from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Say.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our family, my husband Jerry in the center. To his right is myself. Next to myself is Nicole, our daughter. Next to my husband is Sharmaine. And next to Sharmaine is Len, our youngest son. And the picture was taken in 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. These are our grandchildren, my son's five daughters-- Michelle, Kathleen, Kristen, Deborah, and Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: From left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: From left to right, Michelle being on the left. And this was taken in 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture is of myself, at the height of my profession, about five years ago, which is in 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. This picture [BACKGROUND NOISE] was taken in New York, when I was a model in the garment industry. And the year was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect.
